message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Task #3 DoD update ready ÔÇö consensus needed">
**Task #3 is ready for consensus review.** Commit `a70069c` ÔÇö Update Definition of Done with e2e user journey requirements.

Kent Beck updated TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md in 6 places:

**1. DoD Product section** ÔÇö Added:
- "User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature being delivered"
- "All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)"

**2. DoD Engineering section** ÔÇö Added:
- "`npx playwright test` passes -- browser-based e2e tests pass for affected user flows"

**3. Testing Strategy** ÔÇö Added End-to-end tests subsection: Playwright in `e2e/`, role-based locators, "keep this layer thin"

**4. CI/CD Quality Gates** ÔÇö Added `npx playwright test` as step 4

**5. Atomic Green Step Pipeline** ÔÇö Added `npx playwright test` as step 5, renumbered 6-10

**6. Section 7 CI/CD** ÔÇö Added `npx playwright test` to pipeline stages, noted Node.js 22

All changes are minimal ÔÇö no new sections, just additions into existing structures. This is a team process change, so it needs 9/9 consensus. Please reply with CONSENT or CONCERNS.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Let me read the actual changes to verify they match the summary before giving consent.

   100Ôćĺ      user flows

file.content:
## 2. Definition of Done

A feature is **done** when ALL of the following are true:

### Product
- [ ] The user problem is clearly solved
- [ ] All acceptance criteria from the user story are met
- [ ] A first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating (Trunk Test)
- [ ] User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature
      being delivered
- [ ] All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)
- [ ] Edge cases are handled gracefully (not just the happy path)
- [ ] The feature is deployable -- not just merged, but shippable to real users

### Engineering
- [ ] Tests are written first (TDD: Red-Green-Refactor) and all pass
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test` passes (unit + integration)
- [ ] `npx playwright test` passes -- browser-based e2e tests pass for affected
      user flows
- [ ] Error cases return meaningful HTML responses with structured logging
- [ ] Tracing spans cover the new functionality
- [ ] Database migrations (if any) are committed and `sqlx-data.json` is updated

### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)
- [ ] HTML is semantic and uses correct elements (`<button>`, `<label>`, `<ul>`, etc.)
- [ ] All form inputs have visible `<label>` elements (no placeholder-only inputs)
- [ ] All interactive elements are fully keyboard operable
- [ ] Screen reader testing (VoiceOver, NVDA, or equivalent) confirms: interactive
      elements are announced correctly, state changes are communicated, and dynamic
      content updates are announced via live regions
- [ ] ARIA used only when native HTML semantics are insufficient (with comments explaining why)
- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI)
- [ ] Color is never the only indicator of state (always a secondary visual cue)
- [ ] Focus management is correct after HTMX swaps (see HTMX Focus Protocol below)
- [ ] Custom `:focus-visible` styles are present with sufficient contrast
- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected for all animations/transitions

### Design
- [ ] Visual design follows the established design token system
- [ ] All states are designed: empty, loading, error, success
- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear -- one primary focal point per screen
- [ ] Spacing is consistent and uses the defined scale

### Frontend
- [ ] Core functionality works via standard HTML forms and links (progressive
      enhancement -- HTMX and TypeScript enhance but are not required for basic operation)
- [ ] HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior (self-describing elements)
- [ ] TypeScript (if any) is non-critical and has accessible alternatives

242:- **End-to-end tests** (`e2e/` directory): Playwright browser tests against the
243-  running application. Cover critical user journeys (register, login, core feature,
244-  logout). Use role-based and label-based locators (`getByRole`, `getByLabel`) so
245:  tests double as accessibility assertions. Keep this layer thin -- only add e2e
246-  tests for flows that integration tests cannot adequately verify.
247-- **Accessibility testing** (four layers):
--

content.text: Good -- all the additions are where they should be. Let me send my consent.

input.summary: Marty consents to Task #3 DoD updates
input.content:
Marty here with my review of the TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md updates for Task #3.

**CONSENT: Approved.**

These additions codify lessons we just learned from Task #1 (dead-end index page) and Task #2 (Playwright e2e). Specifically:

1. **"User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature being delivered"** -- This is the most important addition. It forces us to think about the product as a connected experience, not isolated screens. The dead-end index page would have been caught by this criterion before it shipped. That's exactly how a DoD should work -- encoding hard-won lessons as future guardrails.

2. **"All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)"** -- Simple, obvious in hindsight, and now we won't forget it. Good.

3. **`npx playwright test` in the Engineering checklist and CI gates** -- Embedding e2e tests into the pipeline means they're not optional. They run on every change. This is the right level of rigor for protecting the user journey.

4. **"Keep this layer thin"** in the testing strategy -- Important constraint. E2e tests are slow and brittle if overused. The guidance to only add e2e tests for flows that integration tests cannot adequately verify is the right call. We don't want 50 Playwright tests -- we want the critical 3-5 that validate the real user experience.

5. **Additions are minimal and in-place** -- No new sections, no restructuring. The changes slot naturally into the existing document structure. That's the right way to evolve team agreements: incrementally, based on evidence.

From a product management perspective, I'm particularly glad to see the "full journey from the landing page" criterion. It aligns with a core principle: we ship connected experiences, not isolated features. Every feature we build should be reachable and usable through the natural entry points of the application.

No concerns. Ready to push.

content.text:
**CONSENT: Approved** for Task #3.

The DoD updates codify lessons from Tasks #1 and #2 as future guardrails. The most valuable addition from a product perspective is "User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature being delivered" -- this forces us to think about connected experiences, not isolated screens. The dead-end index page would have been caught by this criterion before it ever shipped. That's exactly how a Definition of Done should evolve: incrementally, based on evidence.
